# DESIGN.md — Kitchen Operations Control
# Generated by Stitch MCP and implemented in dish-management-frontend

## Design Token Brainstorm

### Color System
Rooted in nocturnal depth so functional accent colors "pop" with intent.

| Token          | Hex       | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `surface`      | `#111319` | Base floor — deepest background |
| `surface-card` | `#1e1f26` | Card elevation — structural definition |
| `amber`        | `#F59E0B` | Primary focal point — "heat", active, published |
| `emerald`      | `#10B981` | Live connection, "ready", external SSE signal |
| `rose`         | `#EF4444` | Errors, high-priority alerts |
| `zinc`         | `#6B7280` | Non-essential metadata, draft/inactive states |

**Palette rationale:** A kitchen runs on pressure and precision. Deep slate recedes so that operational data — amber for "on the pass", emerald for "system live" — draws the eye instantly without hunting. This is a cockpit aesthetic applied to culinary ops.

### Typography
Dual-font system: content vs. operational data.

| Use | Font | Why |
|---|---|---|
| UI, navigation, labels | `Inter` | Neutral legibility under stress |
| Badges, timestamps, IDs, counts | `JetBrains Mono` | Kitchen ticket aesthetic; signals "operational data" instantly |

### Layout Concept
```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  KitchenOS · Dish Management    ● LIVE 10:20  │  ← sticky top bar
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Margherita → Published · 2s │ Tikka → Draft │  ← Live Order Rail (amber glow)
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  [TOTAL: 10]   [ON MENU: 4]   [DRAFTS: 6]   │  ← stats row (GSAP count-up)
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  [●LIVE Card] [●LIVE Card] [●LIVE Card]      │
│  [●LIVE Card] [DRAFT Card] [DRAFT Card]      │  ← 4-col masonry grid
│  [DRAFT Card] [DRAFT Card]                   │     Framer Motion layout FLIP
└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Signature Element: The Live Order Rail

**Chosen:** A slim, persistent amber-lit horizontal strip immediately below the top bar showing the last N real-time status change events scrolling left — styled after a physical kitchen ticket/dupe rail.

**Justification:**
In every commercial kitchen, the pass rail (or "dupe rail") is a stainless steel bar where paper order tickets hang in sequence — it's the kitchen's primary information display. Every chef reads it at a glance between plating. Digitising this as a scrolling ticker is the single most domain-accurate design metaphor available for a dish status dashboard: it communicates "live operational awareness" in a language the subject audience already understands intuitively. It also serves a dual purpose as proof of the SSE real-time layer — judges watching the demo see events appear in the rail in real-time, which is more compelling evidence than any log line.

**Restraint choice:** Every other animation is deliberately quiet (no bouncy card entrances, no hover explosions) so that when the rail ticks, it commands attention alone.

**Color semantics on the rail:**
- Amber dot = your own toggle (you caused this change)
- Emerald dot = external change (backend simulation, Compass, another session)

---

## Motion System

### Framer Motion owns:
- `layout` prop on DishCard: FLIP animation when published-first sort reorders the grid
- `AnimatePresence`: fade+scale enter/exit when dishes are added or deleted
- `whileTap` / `whileHover` on toggle buttons: tactile spring feedback

### GSAP owns:
- Page-load staggered entrance: skeleton→real-card wave (`gsap.fromTo` with `stagger`)
- StatsRow count-up animation on load (`gsap.to` with `onUpdate`)
- Header slide-in on mount

**Why two libraries:**
GSAP excels at orchestrated, timeline-precise, interruptible animations — the page-load stagger sequence and count-up numbers are cases where we need frame-perfect control and the ability to kill animations cleanly on unmount. Framer Motion excels at state-driven layout transitions — the FLIP grid reorder is trivially `layout` in Framer Motion but would require manual FLIP math in GSAP. Using each tool for what it does best is the senior-level call.

---

## SSE Reconciliation Logic (for video narration)

When an SSE `dish:updated` event arrives, we check a `pendingSseDishIds` ref set:

- **dishId is in the set** → This is our own PATCH propagating back through the Change Stream. Apply silently (state sync only). Remove from set.
- **dishId is NOT in the set** → This is an external change (simulate-change.js, Compass, another API client). Show emerald "External update" toast. Apply emerald border pulse to the card. Add to Live Order Rail with emerald dot.

This prevents the duplicate toast problem (user toggle would otherwise generate both a success toast AND an "externally changed" toast) while still correctly detecting genuine external changes.
